## DATA-DRIVEN EVALUATION OF TRAINING ACTION SPACE FOR REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Training action space selection for reinforcement learning (RL) is conflict-prone due to complex state-action relationships. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a Shapley-inspired methodology for training action space categorization and ranking. To reduce exponential-time shapley computations, the methodology includes a Monte Carlo simulation to avoid unnecessary explorations. The effectiveness of the methodology is illustrated using a cloud infrastructure resource tuning case study. It reduces the search space by 80% and categorizes the training action sets into dispensable and indispensable groups. Additionally, it ranks different training actions to facilitate high-performance yet cost-efficient RL model design. The proposed data-driven methodology is extensible to different domains, use cases, and reinforcement learning algorithms.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A reinforcement learning (RL) agent learns how to map states to actions in order to maximize a long-term cumulative award signal in a given environment. Figure 1 shows the various artifacts of an RL algorithm. An RL problem is defined by a quartet of ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ), where _S_ is a set of states or the state space; _A_ is a set of actions or the action space available to influence _S_ ; _Pa_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) = _Pr_ ( _st_ +1 = _s[′] |st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) is the transition probability which is the probability that action _a_ in state _s_ at time _t_ will lead to state _s[′]_ at time _t_ + 1; and finally, _Ra_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) is the immediate reward signal received after transitioning from state _s_ to state _s[′]_ , due to action _a_ . An RL agent training involves recognizing an optimal policy function, _π[∗]_ : _a ← s_ , from a corpus of _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ to maximize the long-term cumulative reward,[�] _Ra_ . The reward function, _Ra_ , is defined ab initio for an efficient goal accomplishment. The transition probability or state-action mapping is defined by the environment dynamics. In many real-life use cases, the agent cannot directly sense the effect of its actions on the environment. This is particularly true when the state-action relationship cannot be modeled by either closed-form analytical expressions [26, 29, 58] or explicit rules as in the popular games such as Chess [28], Go [47, 46], and Atari [34]. This challenge is well documented in the literature [51, 57]. To address this challenge for RL model training [52, 33], simulation-based action models [50] play a pivotal role. The optimal choice of simulation parameters such as the training action space is a non-trivial challenge because of the curse of dimensionality [17], non-linearity [11], and non-uniform action set [25].

Training data valuation [22, 10] and associated artisanal software engineering efforts constitute a large part of the machine learning (ML) life cycle (or MLOps). Yet, most research and development efforts [36] focus on algorithms and infrastructure. Production-grade MLOps needs to handle data lifecycle management (DLM) [40] challenges including: fairness and bias in labeled datasets [16], data quality [9], limitations of benchmarks [42], and reproducibility concerns [39]. For RL, the DLM challenges are further compounded due to complexities arising from non-linear state-action interactions, partiallyobservable processes, non-isometric action spaces [32], and strong domain specificity [21] of action models. With the recent emphasis on ML explainability, traditional supervised learning and deep learning research communities are actively working on systematic data-driven frameworks [14, 53] for training data valuation. We need equivalent frameworks for RL to streamline conflict-prone training action selection [8, 37, 18]. Depending on the agent-environment interactions, different training actions have different relative contributions to the RL agent performance. Some training actions are indispensable because of their unique positions in the parametric space. Other dispensable actions have different relative contributions to the reward function. _Remarkably, a high-cardinality_

_training action space in many cases leads to lower cumulative reward than a well-designed training action space._ To the best of authors’ knowledge, there is hardly any data-driven tool for the evaluation of training action space for RL. Such a tool leads to superior RL agent performance, lower model training and maintenance cost, and strong multi-disciplinary collaboration [35].

This paper proposes a shapley-inspired [54] algorithm to categorize and rank training action sets. It also assists in recognizing cut-off cardinality for the training action space to reduce unnecessary exploration and ensure polynomial time complexity. While Section 2 describes the algorithms for RL training action space evaluation, Section 3 illustrates the effectiveness of the algorithms in a specific case study. Section 4 discusses the relevant work. Finally, Section 5 presents a summary with possible future directions.

## 2 ALGORITHM

The proposed framework provides a shapley-inspired methodology [49] to efficiently filter out unnecessary training actions and discover a near-optimal action set. Figure 2 shows the framework consists of two algorithms and how these two algorithms interact with each other to design a nearoptimal RL model by training action space filtering and evaluation. The first algorithm computes cutoff cardinality for the training action space. The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed in a training action set to enable high-fidelity predictions within an acceptable computational complexity and an error bound. The second algorithm takes action points only above the cut-off cardinality and categorize action points into two classes: _{dispensable, indispensable}_ and ranking dispensable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards. Indispensable action points are absolute essential action points for the RL training to achieve the desired goal. On the other hand, dispensable action points are non-essential action points that can be discarded. However, the RL agent might perform sub-optimally without a disposable action point. By ranking disposable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards, the relative contributions of different training action points are estimated.

## 2.1 CUT-OFF CARDINALITY COMPUTATION FOR TRAINING ACTION POINTS

RL deals with compilation of a quartet ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) and computation of an optimal policy, _π[∗]_ which maximizes the long-term cumulative reward. The search space for a shapley-inspired technique needs to span across the power set for the training action space which has exponential complexity with training action points. To circumvent this problem, the proposed algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality. The inputs to this algorithm include an RL algorithm, the power set for the training action space, the corresponding state space,

the transition probability, an acceptable performance margin, and an acceptable number of iterations. The output is the cut-off cardinality. The algorithm uses an iterative procedure to train the RL agent with different numbers of training actions, starting from all available training actions to only one training action. If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one. The cut-off cardinality signifies computational efficiency — a larger cut-off cadinality means lower computational overhead and vice versa.

**Algorithm 1** Monte Carlo method for cut-off cardinality computation for the training action space **Input:** The RL algorithm, _R_ , the power set for the training action space, _P_ ( _A_ ), the corresponding state space, _P_ ( _S|A_ ), transition probability _**P**_ , an acceptable performance margin, _ϵ_ , and an acceptable number of iterations, _n_ . **Output:** Cut-off cardinality, _|A|cutoff_ **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) **if** the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_ with _n_ iterations **then** _status ← success_ **else** _status ← failure_ **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted. **if** all statuses for a given cardinality, _k_ , are _failures_ **then** _|A|cutoff ← k_ + 1 **end if end for**

## 2.2 CATEGORIZATION AND RANKING OF TRAINING ACTIONS

Algorithm 2 offers a principled procedure for training action selection. As for inputs, it takes the combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality, determined by Algorithm 1, the RL algorithm, the reward function, the threshold condition, and the acceptable number of iterations. As for outputs, it predicts a categorization vector which classifies training action points into two classes: { _dispensable_ , _indispensable_ } and ranks dispensable training actions based on the corresponding cumulative reward. At its core, Algorithm 2 determines whether the policy determined from the given state-action pairs achieves the threshold condition within the acceptable number of iterations. The algorithm uncovers the indispensability of some training action sets for a given RL task. A training action set is deemed to be indispensable: _iff_ upon removal of that training action

The dispensable action points are rank listed in _L_ based on the corresponding cumulative reward. Essentially, _L_ informs the performance impact of different disposable action points on the RL agent.

**Input:** All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality: _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ , the RL algorithm, _R_ , the reward function, the threshold condition, and the acceptable number of iterations. **Output:** Categorization vector, _V_ , which classifies training action points into two classes: { _dispensable_ , _indispensable_ }. ## 2.3 ACTION SHAPLEY

The proposed framework adopts Action Shapley which introduces a formulation for the problem of equitable training action valuation in reinforcement learning. Reinforcement learning has three key building blocks. The first building block is the training action space, _A_ , the corresponding state space _S_ , and a pre-assigned reward function, _Ra_ . The second building block is a reinforcement learning algorithm, _R_ , which is treated as a black box. It takes ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) and computes an optimal policy function, _π[∗]_ . The third building block is the performance measure or the cumulative sum of reward,[�] _Ra_ . The goal of the framework is to compute the valuation for each training action point, _φi_ ( _A, R,_[�] _Ra_ ) as a function of three building blocks. A shapley technique is useful for action valuation because it satisfies nullity, symmetry, and linearity [45]. From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:

where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant. We call _φi_ the Action Shapley value of action _ai_ . Equation 1 suggests Action Shapley computation is with exponential time complexity w.r.t. the training action points. This exponential computational complexity warrants the need for the cut-off cardinality discussed in Algorithm 1.

## 3 CASE STUDY

In this section, we design a resource tuning example (in the cloud) to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm for the training action space evaluation. We evaluate the performance of an MDP [20]-based RL agent, as shown in Figure 1. The state-action mapping and transition probability are modeled using time-series auto-regression [38] after a PCA-based dimensionality reduction [41] with time complexity _O_ ( _klogk_ ), where _k_ is the number of principal components. The choice of the algorithms is purely driven by the nature of the training dataset. A more complex non-linear dataset warrants more complex sequential models such as long short-term memory (LSTM) [44] or attention based models [30]. For action updates, WLOG, an RL-based PID controller [1, 13] is used. Similar to time-series modeling, action update can be conducted by other policy learning algorithms such as SARSA [19].

As shown in Table 1, the objective of the RL agent for this case study is to quickly reduce high CPU utilization below a pre-assigned threshold for a given workload. In most infrastructure/cloud resource tuning technologies [55], CPU utilization represents a key metric. Therefore, the state space for this RL case study consists of virtual machine CPU utilization (%) metrics and the action space is defined by the VM resource set points, i.e., (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB)). The RL agent uses AWS boto3 SDK [2] to manipulate actions and AWS CloudWatch [3] for state space monitoring. The reward is defined as the number of time steps required by the agent to accomplish the objective multiplied by _−_ 1. The negative reward per time step was meant to push the agent to accomplish the task as fast as possible.

The training data for this case study was generated internally [4] with an open source library, _stress_ (https://linux.die.net/man/1/stress). It uses a rectangular workload. The peak of the workload uses the stress command: _sudo stress –io 4 –vm 2 –vm-bytes 1024M –timeout 500s_ . Essentially, the peak is running 4 _I/O_ stressors and 2 VM workers spinning on malloc with 1024 MB per worker for 500 s. The simulated rectangular workload has a time period of 600 s: a high-stress phase of 500 s is followed by an inactive phase of 100 s. The RL training action space is spanned by the power set drawn from the five pairs of EC2 configuration set points as shown in Table 2. Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to 81.25% reduction in the search space. For each EC2 instance in the training action space, the corresponding state metrics, i.e., CPU utilization (%) are shown in Figure 3. The training data was collected for a 24 hour period with 1 minute sampling interval. Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.

With this set up, different RL models are developed with different training action sets and the corresponding rewards and ranks are noted in Table 4. Remarkably, a high cardinality training action set does not guarantee the best reward: the agent with all training actions, _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , does not yield the highest reward. In fact, the action set with _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward. This pattern could be attributed to the state-action interaction in this particular case study. First, the parametric distances

As shown in Table 4, the reward scores can indeed be used for ranking different training action sets leading to a data-driven approach for training action selection. As shown in Figure 5, _large t3a_ is an indispensable element in the training action space for the given RL agent. Without this training action, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal of bringing the CPU utilization below the critical threshold. Similar observations can be made about _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ .

.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Shapley value was postulated in a classic paper in game theory [15] and influences the field of economics significantly [43]. Various shapley-inspired techniques have been applied to model diverse problems including voting [12], resource allocation [48] and bargaining [27]. Recent years saw increased application of shapley-inspired methodologies in machine learning feature importance evaluation and training data valuation [14, 10]. To the best of our knowledge, Shapley value has not been used to quantify training action valuation in a reinforcement learning context. The application of shapley-inspired technique for reinforcement learning is a challenging problem. First, the relationship between state-action pairs and transition probabilities are expensive to model. Also, often the RL training action points are not uniformly positioned. Finally, we have purposefully focused on a small sample cloud infrastructure use case instead of cliched RL use cases [5]. That is because the problem of optimal action selection has relatively more expensive in the cloud infrastructure space where the state-action relationship is only partially observable.

## 5 CONCLUSION

This paper proposes a data-driven methodology for training action space evaluation for RL. The methodology offers a principled framework for training action space categorization and ranking within a finite computational time. It unleashes a strategy for superior model performance and lower modeling cost. Additionally, the proposed methodology is completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms. Therefore, it is a general-purpose methodology extensible to different domains including distributed computing, network traffic control, healthcare, automatic locomotion, building management system, and industrial controls, and different machine learning algorithms such as PCA, Autoencoder, ARIMA, LSTM, Transformer, PID, SARSA, DQN, and many others. For the next phase of the development for this data-agnostic methodology, we are planning to contribute a general RL design library to relevant open source projects [6, 5, 7].

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and timely contribution by addressing a critical yet underexplored challenge in reinforcement learning: the data-driven evaluation and optimization of training action spaces. The core idea—using a Shapley-inspired methodology to assess the marginal contributions of individual actions, combined with Monte Carlo simulation to mitigate exponential complexity—is conceptually strong and aligns well with recent trends in interpretable and efficient RL. The motivation is clearly articulated, particularly in highlighting how high-cardinality action spaces can paradoxically degrade performance and increase cost, which underscores the practical relevance of the work. However, the paper currently suffers from several high-level issues that significantly affect its clarity and scientific rigor. Most notably, the exposition is marred by incomplete sentences, abrupt truncations (especially at the end of Section 2.1), and inconsistent terminology (e.g., interchangeably using "action points" and "actions" without clarification), which undermines the technical coherence. Furthermore, while the Shapley value framework is invoked as a theoretical foundation, the paper lacks a precise formalization of how it is adapted to the RL training action selection context—specifically, how value attribution is computed over subsets of actions, and what the underlying payoff function is. The description of Algorithm 1 is insufficiently detailed to assess correctness or reproducibility, and the claim of polynomial-time complexity due to Monte Carlo sampling requires stronger justification. Additionally, the absence of pseudocode or a step-by-step walkthrough weakens the methodological transparency. Despite these issues, the direction is promising, particularly the dual focus on categorization (dispensable vs. indispensable) and ranking of actions, as well as the demonstrated application in a real-world cloud tuning scenario. To strengthen the paper, the authors must provide a complete, mathematically precise formulation of their framework, fully describe their algorithms, and clarify how their approach improves upon or differs from existing feature or instance selection methods in RL and beyond.

comments.title: Ambiguous and inconsistent handling of cut-off cardinality in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by ϵ within n iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: Multiple issues converge on a central flaw in the definition and implementation of cut-off cardinality in Algorithm 1. The prose claims the cut-off is the 'minimum number of action points needed', yet the algorithm assigns |A|_cutoff = k + 1 when all sets of size k fail—meaning k+1 is the smallest size that may still work. However, this assumes monotonic degradation with decreasing cardinality, which the results contradict (e.g., full set performs worse than some subsets). More critically, the algorithm tests from |A| down and stops at the first k where all statuses are failure, but this does not guarantee that smaller sets won't work, nor does it validate whether size alone determines success. Success is subset-dependent—Table 4 shows some size-4 sets succeed and others fail—yet the method outputs a single cardinality threshold. The condition 'all statuses are failures' is underspecified: it's unclear how many or which subsets per k are tested, and whether 'all' means all possible or just all sampled. The claim of Monte Carlo sampling to reduce complexity is not reflected in the pseudocode, which implies exhaustive enumeration over subsets, making it exponentially costly. The method lacks specification of subset selection, stopping criteria, and sampling protocol. Furthermore, the text alternates between 'up to', 'above', and '≥' the cut-off, creating confusion about which sets are analyzed. The case study uses sets of size ≥4 (6 total), supporting the intended use of 'above' to mean '≥', but 'above' linguistically implies >, introducing inconsistency. Together, these issues reveal that Algorithm 1 is underspecified, potentially irreproducible, and conceptually flawed in reducing a subset-dependent performance metric to a single cardinality threshold. This is a major methodological gap.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 incorrectly assigns indispensability to sets rather than actions
comments.quote: _V_ ( _**A** − a_i_ ) ← indispensable
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 evaluates the impact of removing an action a_i by testing performance on the reduced action set **A** − a_i. If the agent fails, it assigns 'indispensable' to V(**A** − a_i)—i.e., the set without a_i—when logically a_i itself should be labeled indispensable. The categorization vector V is claimed to classify individual training action points (as shown in Table 3), but the pseudocode indexes V by action sets, not actions. This creates a fundamental mismatch between algorithmic output and stated goal. The definition 'a training action set is deemed indispensable iff upon removal... failure' is also ill-posed, as it misattributes the property to the *set after removal* rather than the *removed action*. The correct logic should be: if removal of a_i causes failure, then V(a_i) ← indispensable. As written, the algorithm produces a labeling over sets, not actions, making it impossible to generate the per-action dispensability ranking central to the paper's contribution. While the intent is clear from context and tables, the formal algorithm is logically incorrect and would mislead implementation. This is a major flaw in the core methodology.

comments.title: Contradictory and ambiguous input scope for Algorithm 2
comments.quote: As for inputs, it takes the combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality
comments.explanation: The input to Algorithm 2 is described inconsistently: the text says 'above the cut-off cardinality', while the algorithm block says 'up to cut-off cardinality'. 'Above' (>) and 'up to' (≤) are logical opposites. The case study analyzes six sets: five of size 4 and one of size 5, with cut-off = 4. This implies the intended input is sets of size ≥4, i.e., 'at or above'. However, the phrase 'above' suggests only size >4 (i.e., size 5), and 'up to' suggests size ≤4—neither matching the actual use. The term 'combinations... above' is therefore incorrect and misleading. Given that Algorithm 2 performs leave-one-out testing on high-performing sets, it should logically take sets of size at least the cut-off. The contradiction between text and pseudocode, combined with imprecise terminology, creates ambiguity about which action sets are evaluated, jeopardizing reproducibility. This issue is moderate in severity due to contextual resolution in the case study, but remains a significant logical inconsistency.

comments.title: Missing algorithm reference and methodological gap in step-size determination
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??**, the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0.1
comments.explanation: The placeholder '**??**' indicates a missing algorithm reference, but more importantly, no algorithm or method is described for determining the optimal step-size of 0.1. The paper presents Algorithms 1 and 2 for cut-off determination and action categorization but does not explain how a continuous hyperparameter like step-size is optimized. The step-size critically affects RL performance, yet its selection is introduced without justification—was it via grid search, tuning, or part of the proposed framework? The claim implies such an algorithm exists in the paper, but none is provided. This omission undermines reproducibility and represents a methodological gap, as the full pipeline for data-driven evaluation is incomplete. Multiple issues flag this same gap, confirming it as a substantive underspecification.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of Shapley value with arbitrary constant in theoretical formulation
comments.quote: From game theory, φ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1: where, the sum is over all subsets of A not containing i and C is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to use a Shapley value formulation but introduces 'C is an arbitrary constant', which contradicts the fundamental definition of Shapley values. The Shapley value is uniquely determined by marginal contributions and has no free parameters. The inclusion of an arbitrary constant invalidates the uniqueness, fairness, and interpretability properties that make Shapley values theoretically sound. While the actual implementation uses Monte Carlo approximation and leave-one-out testing (which approximates marginal contributions), the theoretical framing is incorrect. Even if Equation 1 is missing due to OCR, the prose description explicitly references a constant, suggesting a misunderstanding or misrepresentation. This mischaracterization undermines the paper's claim of a principled, game-theoretic foundation. This is a major issue in the theoretical justification, though the empirical method may still hold.

comments.title: Reward value mismatch in Figure 4(c) caption

comments.title: Incorrect claim about computational efficiency and cut-off cardinality
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality signifies computational efficiency — a larger cut-off cardinality means lower computational overhead and vice versa.
comments.explanation: This claim inverts the correct relationship. Cut-off cardinality is defined as the *minimum* number of actions needed for high-fidelity prediction. A larger cut-off (e.g., 5 vs. 2) means *more* actions are required, implying a larger search space and higher computational cost, not lower. Therefore, a larger cut-off should correlate with *higher* overhead, not lower. This is a serious conceptual error that misrepresents the scalability of the method. The authors may have confused 'cut-off' with a pruning threshold, but as stated, it contradicts intuitive and formal notions of efficiency. This undermines the paper's contribution in reducing computational burden.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of cut-off cardinality in Algorithm 1 vs. text description
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: The prose description of Algorithm 1 states that the cut-off cardinality is assigned when all statuses for a given cardinality k are failures, and then sets |A|_cutoff = k + 1. However, Algorithm 1 loops from |A| down to 1, and terminates at the first k where all statuses are failures. This implies that |A|_cutoff = k + 1 is the last cardinality at which the agent still succeeded. But the stated goal is to find the *minimum* number of actions needed for high-fidelity prediction. The logic seems correct: we're identifying the smallest size at which performance breaks down, then setting the cut-off to one more than that. However, the phrase 'minimum number of action points needed' in Section 2 contradicts this: if k is the largest size at which performance *fails*, then k+1 is the smallest size at which it *succeeds*, which matches 'minimum number needed'. But the algorithm tests from |A| downward and stops at the first failure, meaning it may not evaluate all smaller sets thoroughly. The repeat loop 'until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted' suggests that all subsets of size k are tested, but given that the power set is exponential, this is computationally infeasible unless only a Monte Carlo sample is used — which is implied by the use of Monte Carlo but not explicit in the algorithm. Thus, the ambiguity lies in whether the algorithm tests all subsets of size k or just a sample. The claim that it reduces search space by 80% in the case study supports that it uses sampling. However, the pseudocode does not reflect sampling: it implies full enumeration. This underspecifies the method — a reader cannot reproduce the Monte Carlo aspect from Algorithm 1 as written.

The notation <si, ai> suggests a single state-action pair, but the operation inside the loop removes ai from the action space and evaluates V(**A** − ai). This implies leave-one-out evaluation, which is consistent with Shapley value computation. However, the input is combinations *up to* cut-off cardinality, while Section 2.1 computes a cut-off *below which* results are unstable. The text says analysis focuses on sets *above* cut-off cardinality, yet Algorithm 2 inputs sets 'up to' cut-off cardinality, which contradicts. But 'up to' usually means ≤, which would be size ≤ 4. This is a direct contradiction. Given Table 4 includes the full set (size 5) and several size-4 sets, and the paper states 'power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial results', the intended meaning is sets of size *at least* 4. Thus, 'up to' is incorrect and should be 'at least' or 'above'. This ambiguity could mislead a reader into thinking smaller sets are being evaluated, which would contradict the stated purpose. comments.title: Cut-off cardinality definition contradicts its computational interpretation
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed... The algorithm uses an iterative procedure to train the RL agent with different numbers of training actions, starting from all available training actions to only one training action.
comments.explanation: The definition says cut-off cardinality is the *minimum* number needed for high-fidelity prediction. But the algorithm starts from the full set and decreases k until performance fails, then sets |A|_cutoff = k + 1. So if performance fails at k=3, cutoff is 4. This is consistent with the minimum size that works. However, the phrase 'minimum number needed' could be misinterpreted as the smallest possible size that still works — which is what it is. However, the algorithm does not guarantee that: it only checks sizes from full down to 1, and stops at the first failure. It does not confirm that no smaller set (e.g., k=3.5 is not possible, but non-contiguous subsets of size 2) might work. The cut-off is based on contiguous reduction from full set, but action subsets are arbitrary. The method assumes monotonicity — that smaller sets perform worse — which may not hold due to overfitting or interference. The case study shows that the full set performs worse than a subset, which contradicts monotonicity. So the algorithm could fail to identify the true minimum working size. More importantly, the text implies cut-off is a strict threshold below which performance breaks, but the results show that some size-4 subsets fail (Table 4), while others succeed. So performance depends on *which* actions are included, not just cardinality. Therefore, defining cut-off cardinality as a single number based solely on size is flawed when success is subset-dependent. The algorithm computes a heuristic, but presenting it as 'the' cut-off cardinality oversimplifies.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Shapley value formulation
comments.quote: From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1: where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: Equation 1 is missing from the provided text, but the prose describes a Shapley value formulation with an arbitrary constant C. In standard Shapley value theory, the value is uniquely determined by the marginal contributions and does not include an arbitrary constant. The Shapley value is defined as a specific weighted average of marginal contributions over all permutations or subsets. The inclusion of 'C is an arbitrary constant' is deeply problematic: it contradicts the uniqueness property of Shapley values. This cannot be an OCR artifact — no standard form includes a free constant. Either the equation was corrupted (e.g., missing normalization), or the authors misunderstand the Shapley value. Given that the paper claims the method is 'shapley-inspired' and acknowledges exponential complexity, it may be that they are proposing a modified version. But without the equation, it's impossible to tell. However, in context, the term 'arbitrary constant' likely results from OCR or drafting error (e.g., misinterpreting a normalization factor). Still, as presented, it invalidates the foundation of Action Shapley. Since Shapley values are central to the methodology, and the constant undermines their uniqueness and interpretability, this is a major issue — but only if the text is taken at face value. Given that the algorithm does not directly implement Shapley but uses Monte Carlo approximation, and the categorization is based on leave-one-out testing (which approximates marginal contribution), the core idea may still hold. Still, the theoretical framing is incorrect.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Cut-off Cardinality in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this description seems to suggest that the cut-off cardinality is assigned as k + 1 when performance fails to improve at cardinality k. This would imply that the smallest *failing* set determines the cut-off, and thus the minimal *successful* set is of size k+1. However, the surrounding prose states that the algorithm starts from |A| and goes down to 1, meaning it tests in decreasing order of cardinality. If performance degrades below a certain k (i.e., fails), then k+1 is chosen as the minimum size that still achieves performance. This aligns with the definition of cut-off cardinality as the minimum number needed for high-fidelity predictions. But the phrasing 'assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one' is ambiguous: is it assigning when failure occurs at k or success? The pseudocode shows that |A|_cutoff ← k + 1 when all statuses for k are failures — meaning that when no set of size k achieves improvement, then k+1 is sufficient. This makes sense: the largest failing size plus one is the minimal working size. However, the text's use of 'improve by _ϵ_' is problematic — it’s not improvement relative to what? No baseline is given. Is it improvement over iterations for the same cardinality, or improvement relative to higher-cardinality sets? Given that the algorithm trains for n iterations per set, it likely refers to convergence stagnation. But without clarifying whether the reward is expected to converge or improve *relative to other cardinalities*, this could mislead a reader into thinking it's a within-set convergence criterion, not a between-cardinality threshold. The intended logic appears correct — use Monte Carlo over subsets of decreasing size until performance breaks, take the last sufficient size — but the wording around 'improve by _ϵ_' lacks a reference point and could be misconstrued.

comments.title: Ambiguous Input to Algorithm 2: 'Combinations... above cut-off cardinality'

comments.explanation: The phrase 'above the cut-off cardinality' is ambiguous. Does it mean sets whose cardinality is greater than |A|_cutoff? That would contradict Algorithm 1, which suggests using sets *of size at least* |A|_cutoff, since those are the ones that performed well. But later in the description, Algorithm 2 evaluates the effect of removing a single action from a set — suggesting it works with sets *of* the cut-off cardinality or larger. Moreover, the input says 'up to cut-off cardinality' in the algorithm block, which contradicts the earlier 'above'. Looking at Algorithm 2’s input: 'All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality', which directly contradicts the text’s 'above the cut-off cardinality'. This is a clear inconsistency. 'Above' means >, 'up to' means ≤. They are opposites. Even accounting for OCR, 'upto' is clearly not 'above'. The correct interpretation must be that Algorithm 2 evaluates subsets formed from action sets *of size at least* |A|_cutoff, but more precisely, it likely takes the full set or sets around the cut-off size to test indispensability via leave-one-out. Given the case study uses sets of size 4 and 5, and cut-off is 4, it likely uses sets of size ≥ |A|_cutoff. But the text alternates between 'above' and 'upto', which cannot both be right. This creates confusion on whether Algorithm 2 uses larger or smaller sets than the cut-off. Since the algorithm is central to the claim of 80% search space reduction, this ambiguity affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 Incorrectly Assigns Indispensability to Removed Action
comments.quote: _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← indispensable_
comments.explanation: This line in Algorithm 2 is deeply problematic. The valuation vector V is indexed by _A − ai_, i.e., the action set with ai removed, but the label assigned is 'indispensable' — a property of the *removed* action ai, not the remaining set. If removing ai causes failure, then ai is indispensable. But here, the algorithm assigns V(A\ai) ← indispensable, which suggests the *set* A\ai is indispensable — which contradicts intuition: A\ai is the set *without* ai, so if the agent fails with that set, then A\ai is insufficient, not indispensable. The labeling should instead be on the action ai itself. The categorization vector V should map actions to {dispensable, indispensable}, not sets. The current code assigns tags to sets rather than individual actions, making it impossible to generate a per-action categorization as claimed in Table 3. The surrounding text says 'a training action set is deemed to be indispensable iff upon removal... failure', but this misapplies the term to the *set* after removal, whereas the tables categorize *individual actions*. There's a disconnect: the algorithm outputs V indexed by sets, but the paper presents V as a vector over actions. This is not resolvable by context — it's a flaw in algorithmic design. The intended logic is clear: test each action by removing it; if goal no longer achieved, mark ai as indispensable. But the algorithm does not reflect that. This undermines the core contribution: action categorization.

comments.title: Misuse of 'Shapley' Without Proper Formulation
comments.quote: From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1: where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The paper refers to 'Equation 1' but provides no actual equation — likely omitted by OCR or formatting. The Shapley value in game theory is defined as a weighted average of marginal contributions over all subsets: φ_i = Σ_{S⊆A\{i}} [ |S|!(|A|−|S|−1)! / |A|! ] * [v(S∪{i}) − v(S)] ]. The text mentions the sum is over subsets not containing i and that C is an arbitrary constant — this is inaccurate. There is no 'arbitrary constant' in the Shapley value; it's fully determined by the value function v. The reference to C suggests a misunderstanding or misrepresentation. Even if OCR removed the equation, the description is flawed. While the paper correctly notes exponential complexity, the characterization of Shapley as having an arbitrary constant undermines the uniqueness and fairness guarantees of Shapley values. This is especially problematic because the paper positions 'Action Shapley' as a principled valuation method. Without the correct form, the theoretical foundation is weakened.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 Does Not Match Monte Carlo Method Description
comments.quote: The algorithm uses an iterative procedure to train the RL agent with different numbers of training actions, starting from all available training actions to only one training action.
comments.explanation: The text claims that Algorithm 1 uses a Monte Carlo method, but the pseudocode does not reflect Monte Carlo sampling. Instead, it iterates over cardinalities k from |A| down to 1, and for each k, appears to exhaust all subsets of that size (implied by 'until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted'). This is combinatorial enumeration, not Monte Carlo. A true Monte Carlo approach would sample a subset of sets for each cardinality k, not exhaust them, to avoid exponential cost. Given that the purpose of Algorithm 1 is to *avoid* exponential complexity, full enumeration over the power set contradicts this goal. The method would still be exponential unless only a sample of subsets is evaluated per k. The prose mentions Monte Carlo to reduce complexity, but the algorithm as written does not incorporate sampling — it just loops through cardinalities. This is a significant gap: either the algorithm is misdescribed, or the implementation is not Monte Carlo. The claim of polynomial time in Table 1 is only justifiable if subset sampling is used, but this isn’t stated.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Disposable' vs 'Dispensable'
comments.quote: However, the RL agent might perform sub-optimally without a disposable action point.
comments.explanation: The paper uses both 'dispensable' (defined in intro and used in tables) and 'disposable' (in this quote). OCR might explain this, as 'd' and 's' can be confused, but 'disposable' appears again in the sentence: 'performance impact of different disposable action points'. The correct term used everywhere else is 'dispensable'. 'Disposable' is not a standard synonym in this context and could imply biodegradability or temporary usage, introducing unnecessary confusion. Given that 'dispensable' is formally defined and used in all headings and tables, 'disposable' is likely a typo. However, because it appears twice, it may indicate a broader inconsistency in terminology. Context resolves it — meaning is clear — but still a minor editorial flaw.

comments.title: Underspecified cut-off cardinality computation in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations **then** we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the intent of Algorithm 1 is clear: iteratively reduce the number of training actions and determine the minimal size (cut-off cardinality) beyond which performance plateaus. However, the algorithm description lacks critical specification of how subsets of a given cardinality are selected. The phrase 'all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted' suggests that multiple subsets of size _k_ are evaluated, but it doesn't state how many or which ones. Given the exponential nature of the power set, it's implausible to test all subsets. The use of Monte Carlo simulation implies sampling, but the sampling strategy (e.g., random, stratified) and number of samples per cardinality are not specified. Moreover, the condition 'all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_' raises a concern: does 'all' mean every single sampled subset at size _k_, or a majority? If one subset fails to improve and the rest do, is that sufficient to trigger the cut-off? This level of ambiguity makes it impossible to reproduce the algorithm as described. While the overall goal is reasonable — identifying a minimal effective set size — the lack of procedural detail in subset selection, stopping criteria, and Monte Carlo sampling protocol constitutes a significant gap.

comments.title: Inconsistency in ranking logic between Table 4 and text
comments.quote: the action set with _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward. ... _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward.
comments.explanation: The passage states that the training action set _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward (−13), which is indeed the least negative in Table 4. Then later it says 'the action set with _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward' — but that set has a reward of −16, which is worse. This appears to be a typo. However, checking the context, the earlier sentence likely meant to say 'the action set with _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward', and the later one referencing _<small...>_ in Figure 4(c) is just illustrating another case. But the repetition of 'yields the highest reward' for a different action set suggests a copy-paste or editing error. The problem is not just contradiction: it risks misleading readers about which action set is best. Especially because the claim is central to the paper's argument about high-cardinality not implying high performance, accuracy matters.

comments.title: Algorithm reference missing in text
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The placeholder '??:' is clearly a result of OCR or incomplete copy-editing. The more important concern is that no algorithm in the paper is described for selecting step-sizes or update rules. The paper focuses on action space *selection*, not on tuning continuous update parameters. While it's mentioned that 'an RL-based PID controller' is used, and that optimal step-size is 0.1, there is no explanation of *how* this value was determined. Was it tuned empirically? Is it part of the proposed framework? If so, which part? The framework as described in Algorithms 1 and 2 focuses on discrete action set pruning, not on designing continuous policy updates. This parameter (0.1) significantly affects the RL behavior but is introduced without justification or connection to the proposed methodology. Given that the paper claims to be a general-purpose, data-driven evaluation framework, omitting how such an essential hyperparameter was chosen undermines reproducibility.

comments.title: Misleading characterization of cut-off cardinality's impact on computational efficiency
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality signifies computational efficiency — a larger cut-off cadinality means lower computational overhead and vice versa.
comments.explanation: At first, this seems counterintuitive: one would expect a smaller cut-off cardinality (fewer needed actions) to mean lower overhead. But reading carefully, the authors define cut-off cardinality as the *minimum* number of actions needed for high-fidelity predictions. So a *larger* cut-off suggests that more actions are required, implying higher computational cost. Therefore, stating that 'a larger cut-off cardinality means lower computational overhead' is incorrect — it's the opposite. For example, if |A|_cutoff = 5, you need at least 5 actions; if it were 2, the search space is smaller. The confusion may arise from equating 'cut-off' with pruning threshold, but the logic is inverted here. This is a serious conceptual error because it misrepresents the scalability benefits of the method. While the intention might have been to say that pruning leads to efficiency, the way it's phrased inverts the cause-effect relationship and contradicts standard usage of 'computational efficiency'.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of indispensability in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: A training action set is deemed to be indispensable: _iff_ upon removal of that training action point, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal within a finite number of iterations.
comments.explanation: The language here is ambiguous: is it a single action point or a set that's removed? The text says 'removal of that training action point', which suggests one action, but refers to 'training action set' being indispensable. Later, in Table 3, individual actions like _small t3a_ are categorized as dispensable or indispensable, which supports atomic evaluation. But Algorithm 2 takes 'combinations of training state-action pairs' as input — suggesting evaluation on sets. The actual procedure must be: for each action _i_, remove _i_ and test whether performance still meets threshold. But the algorithm pseudocode checks _V_(**A** − _a_i_), assigning indispensability to **A** − _a_i_, which is the *remaining set*, not to _a_i_. This is problematic: the output should be a categorization of individual actions, but the assignment is to the complement set. For example, _V_(_A_ − _a_i_) ← indispensable associates indispensability with the set missing _a_i_, rather than _a_i_ itself. That would mean the *absence* of _a_i_ leads to success — contradicting the intended definition. This appears to be a critical indexing/assignment bug in the pseudocode, severely affecting the interpretation of the algorithm.

comments.title: Inconsistent reward recording in Figure 4 caption
comments.quote: Figure 4: ... (c) RL loop with a training action set of _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ . The reward is noted to be _−_ 1
comments.explanation: In the main body text, just two paragraphs above, the same action set is reported to have a reward of −16: 'For the third training action set of _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , the reward is _−_ 16'. Table 4 also confirms this, showing −16 for that set. The caption in Figure 4, however, claims the reward is −1. This is a glaring numerical inconsistency, not merely a formatting error. Since the figure is illustrative, and the −1 might be a typo (missing '6'), but without access to the figure itself (only OCR text), it's impossible to know whether this occurs in the original or is an OCR glitch. However, assuming the text version reflects the intended content, this contradiction remains unresolved and directly undermines trust in the reported results. Since −1 vs −16 represents a 1500% difference in performance, the severity is high.

comments.title: Questionable claim about search space reduction
comments.quote: Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to 81.25% reduction in the search space.
comments.explanation: The claim starts by saying the training action space is the power set of five actions, which has 2^5 = 32 possible subsets. Then it says the analysis focuses on six training action sets. 6/32 = 18.75%, so the reduction is indeed 81.25% — this calculation checks out numerically. But the reasoning is flawed. The paper says 'the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results', so only sets of size ≥4 are considered. All subsets of size 4 and 5: C(5,4)=5, C(5,5)=1 → total 6, which matches. So far, so good. But Algorithm 1 is supposed to determine cut-off cardinality = k + 1, where performance fails at k. If k=3 fails, then |A|_cutoff = 4. So only sets of size ≥4 are above the cut-off? But the method in Section 2.2 says it evaluates 'combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality' — does that mean strictly above, or ≥? The algorithm text says 'above', which would mean only sets of size >4, i.e., size 5 only. But they evaluate six sets including five of size 4. So 'above' likely means '≥', but this contradicts the English meaning of 'above'. This undermines confidence in the method's consistency. However, given the numerical match, it's more of a semantic ambiguity than a logical flaw.

comments.title: Reward value mismatch in Figure 4(c) description
comments.quote: Figure 4: Examples of dispensable actions. (a) RL loop with all training actions, _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ . The reward is noted to be _−_ 21. (b) RL loop with a training action set of _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ . The reward is noted to be _−_ 13. (c) RL loop with a training action set of _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ . The reward is noted to be _−_ 1
comments.explanation: Initially, I noticed that in the first sentence of Figure 4(c), the reward is written as '−1', which immediately stood out because Table 4 lists the reward for the action set excluding medium t3a (i.e., _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_) as −16. This contradicts both the figure caption and the narrative in Section 3, where this exact configuration is discussed and reported to have a reward of −16. Checking the passage to review, I found that later in the same figure caption, it says 'The reward is noted to be _−_ 16'. This suggests that the first mention ('_−_ 1') is an OCR artifact or typo introduced during PDF conversion. However, the presence of two conflicting values in the same paragraph is misleading and could confuse a reader. Even if OCR is at fault, the final typeset version must resolve this. The correct value, supported by Table 4 and context, is −16. The inconsistency remains problematic because both numbers appear in close proximity.

comments.title: Inconsistent dispensability claim in Figure 4 caption
comments.quote: Both _small t3a_ and _medium t3a_ are noted to be dispensable actions.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this claim aligns with Table 3 and the core argument: both small and medium are labeled dispensable. However, the statement appears immediately after discussing action sets where _medium t3a_ is absent and performance improves (reward −13), or where its absence causes failure (e.g., in Table 4, removing large t3a causes failure, but removing medium t3a in some sets still yields success). The issue arises when considering the action set _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ (reward −16), which lacks medium t3a but succeeds. Yet in another set excluding medium t3a—namely _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_—wait, that’s the same set. The problem is not logical but contextual: the sentence appears to generalize from specific examples to a global claim without qualifying that dispensability is defined via failure upon removal in *all* contexts. But Algorithm 2 defines indispensability as failure to achieve the goal upon removal; dispensability allows suboptimal but not failed outcomes. So while the claim is broadly correct, it risks misinterpretation: the caption suggests both are dispensable as a flat conclusion from these figures, but Figure 5 shows that missing large t3a causes failure—yet it doesn’t test removing only medium t3a in isolation. The support for medium t3a’s dispensability is indirect. A careful reader might wonder: was a controlled ablation done on medium t3a alone? Table 4 provides such evidence (e.g., _<medium t3a, large, xlarge, 2xlarge_ does succeed), so the conclusion holds. The caption is therefore acceptable, but could be misread as overgeneralizing from the visuals. No actual error, but slight ambiguity.

comments.title: Missing algorithm reference in case study method description
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The placeholder '**??**' clearly indicates a missing reference, likely from OCR degradation or incomplete redaction. The full paper context does not reveal which algorithm computes the optimal step-size, and no such algorithm is described in Sections 2.1–2.3. The PAPER SUMMARY lists the optimal step-size as a key claim and notes it is 0.1 in both dimensions, but no method is formally described for determining it. This is not resolved by context. While the high-level framework includes valuation and ranking, step-size optimization appears to be an additional component not covered in the presented algorithms. Given that the paper prides itself on methodology completeness, this omission affects reproducibility. A reader cannot reconstruct how the optimal step-size was determined—was it grid search? Gradient probing? Part of the Monte Carlo loop? This constitutes a methodological gap. The authors correctly emphasize data-driven determination, but fail to specify how, making this a real underspecification.

comments.title: Inaccurate reduction percentage claimed in abstract and case study
comments.quote: It reduces the search space by 80% and categorizes the training action sets into dispensable and indispensable groups.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims an 80% reduction, but the full context in Section 3 and PAPER SUMMARY states an 81.25% reduction. The power set of 5 actions has 2^5 = 32 subsets. Excluding those with cardinality <4 means excluding all subsets of size 0,1,2,3. The number of such subsets is C(5,0)+C(5,1)+C(5,2)+C(5,3) = 1+5+10+10 = 26. So 26/32 = 81.25% excluded, meaning search space reduced by 81.25%. The abstract's '80%' is a rounded-down or approximate figure. While minor, this introduces a numerical inconsistency between abstract and detailed sections. In academic writing, such metrics should be consistent across the paper. Although the error is small and the claim 'reduces by ~80%' would be acceptable, stating '80%' when it is precisely 81.25% may mislead readers expecting exactness, especially since the exact number is later reported. The authors correctly compute the correct value later, but the abstract should reflect it.

comments.title: Euclidean distance inconsistency in parametric space
comments.quote: Euclidean distance between _small t3a_ and _medium t3a_ is equal to 2, that between _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ is 16 _._ 5
comments.explanation: Using the data from Table 2: small t3a = (2 vCPU, 2 GB), medium t3a = (2,4), so delta = (0,2), Euclidean distance = √(0² + 2²) = 2 — correct. xlarge t3a = (4,16), 2xlarge t3a = (8,32), delta = (4,16), distance = √(4² + 16²) = √(16 + 256) = √272 ≈ 16.492, which rounds to 16.5 — also correct. So the distances are numerically accurate. However, the paper refers to '(# vCPUs, Memory Size)' space without weighting or normalizing dimensions that have vastly different scales (vCPU range: 2–8, Memory: 2–32). Using raw Euclidean distance here assumes equal importance of a vCPU and a GB of memory, which may not be valid. But since the paper defines 'parametric distance' this way explicitly in the summary and uses it consistently, this is a modeling choice, not an error. The authors are transparent about non-uniform spacing being a problem. No flaw in calculation or presentation — in fact, correctly reflecting a real issue in action space geometry.

comments.title: Inconsistent cut-off cardinality explanation in Algorithm 1 and case study
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations **then** we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused about how the cut-off cardinality is assigned in Algorithm 1. The logic states that if rewards do not improve by ϵ at a certain cardinality k, then status is failure, and the cut-off is set to k + 1. However, in the case study (section 3), the authors note that power sets below cardinality 4 lead to 'trivial/unstable results', and hence focus on sets with ≥4 actions WLOG. That suggests the cut-off cardinality is 4. But according to Algorithm 1, failure at k implies cut-off is k+1. So, if results below 4 are bad, we should see failure at k=3, leading to |A|_cutoff = 4 — which aligns. However, the algorithm loops from |A| down to 1, and assigns cut-off upon first failure. This implies that performance degradation must occur monotonically as cardinality decreases, which is not guaranteed. The paper does not verify whether smaller sets consistently fail or whether the failure condition is met only at k=3. Without evidence that all sets of size 3 fail while all of size 4 succeed, the use of k=4 as cut-off lacks justification. The claim of 81.25% reduction (from 32 to 6 sets) only holds if only the 6 subsets of size 4 are considered — but |P(A)| = 2^5 = 32, subsets of size ≥4 are C(5,4)+C(5,5)=5+1=6, which matches. So the math checks out. However, the algorithmic justification in Algorithm 1 does not clearly lead to excluding all sets below 4 unless every k<4 fails uniformly. The paper assumes this without showing convergence behavior across k. Thus, the reasoning from algorithm to cut-off choice is underspecified.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated reward in Figure 4(c) and Table 4
comments.quote: The reward is noted to be _−_ 1
comments.explanation: In Table 4, the reward for the training action set _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ is listed as −16, and its rank is 2. This is consistent with the text stating it yields the second-best performance. However, in the caption of Figure 4(c), it says 'The reward is noted to be −1'. The −1 is clearly a typo or OCR error, because it contradicts Table 4, the numerical results, and the claim that this set achieves −16. Since the point of Figure 4 is to illustrate comparative performance, labeling it with −1 would mislead readers into thinking it performed almost immediately, which contradicts both the data and the analysis. All other rewards in Figure 4 match Table 4 (−21 and −13). Therefore, this is not an OCR misread of a symbol, but a likely typesetting error in the figure caption. Even though attentive readers can resolve this via cross-referencing Table 4, a careful reviewer must flag that the figure misstates a key result.

comments.title: Ambiguous status condition in Algorithm 1 leads to non-deterministic cut-off
comments.quote: **if** all statuses for a given cardinality, _k_ , are _failures_ **then** _|A|cutoff ← k_ + 1
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 attempts to determine cut-off cardinality by testing subsets of decreasing size k from |A| down to 1. The condition for setting the cut-off is that *all* statuses at cardinality k are failures. But it's unclear what constitutes 'all necessary training action sets' at a given k. For example, with |A|=5, there are C(5,k) subsets of size k. Does the algorithm evaluate all of them? The algorithm says 'until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted', but the loop is over k, not over subsets. This suggests that for each k, it only tests one configuration — but that would be insufficient to generalize across subsets. The cut-off should reflect the *capability* of *any* size-k set to achieve the goal. The case study shows that some size-4 sets succeed (e.g., including _large_, _xlarge_, _2xlarge_) while others fail (e.g., missing _large_). Thus, performance is subset-dependent, not just cardinality-dependent. But Algorithm 1 implies that cut-off depends on *all* sets of size k failing. Since some size-4 sets succeed and some fail, the status would not be uniformly 'failure' at k=4, so cut-off would not be set until k where *all* subsets fail — possibly k=2 or lower. But the authors set cut-off at 4 based on instability below 4. This mismatch suggests that either the algorithm is underspecified, or the implementation differs from what's written. The current description does not clarify whether it tests all subsets per k, aggregates success probabilistically, or uses a representative subset. Without this detail, the method is irreproducible.

comments.title: Incorrect Euclidean distance calculation in case study discussion
comments.quote: While the Euclidean distance between _small t3a_ and _medium t3a_ is equal to 2, that between _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ is 16.5.
comments.explanation: The Euclidean distance should be calculated in the (vCPUs, Memory) space. From Table 2: _small t3a_ = (2,2), _medium t3a_ = (2,4), so distance = √[(2−2)² + (4−2)²] = √4 = 2 — correct. _xlarge t3a_ = (4,16), _2xlarge t3a_ = (8,32), so distance = √[(8−4)² + (32−16)²] = √[16 + 256] = √272 ≈ 16.49 ≈ 16.5 — so the numerical value is correct. Initially I thought '16.5' might be a miscalculation, but it's actually accurate. However, the use of Euclidean distance assumes the two dimensions are on comparable scales, which they are not — vCPUs range 2–8, Memory 2–32 GB. Without normalization, Euclidean distance is dominated by memory. The authors use this unnormalized distance to argue non-uniform spacing hinders RL adoption, but the issue is more about feature scaling than parametric geometry per se. Yet, since they're making a qualitative point about irregular spacing, and the distances are numerically accurate, this is not a technical error. The concern is resolved upon checking the math. No flag needed.

comments.title: Misplaced emphasis on Monte Carlo method for cut-off computation
comments.quote: To reduce exponential-time shapley computations, the methodology includes a Monte Carlo simulation to avoid unnecessary explorations.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that a Monte Carlo simulation is used to avoid exponential cost. But in Algorithm 1, the Monte Carlo method isn't described further — it's just a loop over cardinalities testing RL performance. 'Monte Carlo' typically implies random sampling, but the algorithm iterates deterministically from |A| down to 1, and may evaluate all subsets. If it samples subsets stochastically, that should be specified. The term 'Monte Carlo' here seems misapplied or underspecified. The intent might be to avoid evaluating the full power set by testing only some configurations, but the algorithm doesn't say how many or how they are selected. Without randomization or justification for sampling, calling it 'Monte Carlo' is misleading. The method described is more of a systematic ablation study than a Monte Carlo simulation. While the idea of reducing search via early cut-off is valid, labeling it as Monte Carlo without implementation details risks appearing hand-wavy. This is a framing issue: the approach may work, but the terminology doesn't match standard usage in computational statistics.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 logic incorrectly infers indispensability from removal
comments.quote: **if** Threshold condition is _never_ satisfied _within_ the acceptable number of iterations **then** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← indispensable_
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 evaluates whether removing action ai causes failure, and if so, marks the *set A − ai* as indispensable — but that notation is backward. If removing ai leads to failure, then ai itself is indispensable, not the reduced set. The assignment _V_ (**A** − ai) ← indispensable suggests that the set *without* ai is indispensable, which contradicts the definition: the indispensable *actions* are the ones whose removal breaks performance. Correctly, it should be _V_(ai) ← indispensable. This is a serious notational inconsistency. The caption says it outputs a categorization vector V that classifies training *action points*, implying V is indexed by actions, not sets. But the assignment uses V(**A** − ai), which is a set. This is not due to OCR — the symbol **A** is consistently used for the full set. The condition should be: if π(*S, A − {ai}, Pa, Ra*) fails to meet threshold, then V(ai) ← indispensable. As written, the algorithm misassigns labels to sets instead of actions. This undermines the clarity of Algorithm 2 and could lead to implementation errors. While the intent is clear from context and Table 3 correctly lists individual actions, the formal algorithm is logically flawed.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a timely and significant problem in reinforcement learning: the "curse of dimensionality" and inefficiency caused by poorly scoped training action spaces. By proposing a Shapley-inspired methodology to categorize and rank actions, the authors provide a potentially valuable contribution to MLOps for RL. However, the current manuscript faces clarity and technical precision issues that require attention. Specifically, the introduction contains several syntactical errors and informal phrasing (e.g., "From a corpus of `{ (si, ai) }`" implies a supervised learning paradigm, which contradicts the fundamental exploration-based nature of RL). Furthermore, while the motivation is clear, the paper lacks a rigorous mathematical derivation of why Shapley values—traditionally used for feature attribution—are an appropriate proxy for evaluating the performance contribution of actions in a non-stationary MDP. To strengthen the paper, the authors should clarify the distinction between "training action space" (which implies a set of hyperparameters or tuning parameters) and the "policy action space" (the agent’s behavioral output), as the current formulation conflates these concepts, leaving the exact scope of the proposed intervention ambiguous.

comments.title: OCR Artifacts in Mathematical Notation
comments.quote: maximize the long-term cumulative reward,[] _Ra_ .
comments.explanation: The text contains a stray character '[]' before the reward function symbol, likely resulting from an OCR error related to a summation or integral. This impacts the readability of the optimization objective definition.

comments.title: Terminology Inconsistency: Dispensable vs. Disposable
comments.quote: categorize action points into two classes: {dispensable, indispensable} ... By ranking disposable training action points
comments.explanation: The authors interchangeably use 'dispensable' and 'disposable' to refer to the same classification. This lack of terminological consistency creates ambiguity for the reader when mapping back to the defined categorization vector.

comments.title: Under-specification of the 'Never Satisfied' Condition in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: if Threshold condition is _never_ satisfied _within_ the acceptable number of iterations
comments.explanation: It is not explicitly defined whether 'never satisfied' refers to the cumulative reward failing to cross a threshold or a complete task failure. Given RL training variance, it is unclear if this is a deterministic or stochastic check, impeding reproducibility.

comments.title: Broken Cross-Reference to Step-Size Methodology
comments.quote: Using Algorithm ?? , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 . 1
comments.explanation: The manuscript contains a '??' placeholder for a cross-reference. Furthermore, the paper lacks an explicit algorithm or mathematical derivation for the 0.1 step-size, rendering this empirical claim underspecified.

comments.title: Numerical Inconsistency regarding Reward Values
comments.quote: The reward is noted to be − 1
comments.explanation: There is a direct contradiction between the text/Table 4 (reporting a reward of -16) and the Figure 4 caption (reporting a reward of -1). This should be corrected for accuracy and to avoid reader confusion.

comments.title: Unsupported Search Space Reduction Claim
comments.quote: That amounts to 81.25% reduction in the search space.
comments.explanation: The logic behind the '81.25% reduction' claim is mathematically opaque. The transition from a power set of actions to a focused subset lacks formal justification, and the 'Without Loss of Generality' (WLOG) assertion is not sufficiently substantiated.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Action Shapley Definition and Value Function
comments.explanation: The definition of the value function v(S) is ambiguous. It relates to cumulative reward but is not formally defined in terms of state-action dependencies. Additionally, the summation index notation is inconsistent across the paper, failing to clarify how v(S) is calculated for RL training.

comments.title: Ambiguous Convergence Criteria in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by ϵ within n iterations
comments.explanation: The criteria lacks clarity regarding whether ϵ refers to an absolute reward improvement or a statistical threshold over a window. In the context of stochastic RL, the current formulation is prone to premature labeling of 'failure' due to variance.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Claim regarding Polynomial Complexity
comments.quote: ensure polynomial time complexity.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that Shapley-based categorization achieves polynomial time complexity, but this is an overclaim as they do not provide a defined sample size for the Monte Carlo approximation relative to |A|.

comments.title: Methodological Ambiguity in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: essentially, L informs the performance impact
comments.explanation: The algorithm computes dispensable actions without specifying how to handle dependencies between actions. In non-linear state-action spaces, the order of removal significantly impacts marginal contribution calculation, which is not formally addressed.
